message.content: What is the estimated mass of the supermassive black hole Sagittarius A*?

content.text: I'll look up the current estimated mass of Sagittarius A* for you.

arguments.query: Sagittarius A* supermassive black hole mass estimate 2024 2025

This article is about the black hole. For the surrounding region, see [Sagittarius A](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Sagittarius_A).

Sagittarius A*
https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/File:EHT_Saggitarius_A_black_hole.tif
Sagittarius A* imaged by the [Event Horizon Telescope](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Event_Horizon_Telescope) in 2017, released in 2022

Observation data
[Epoch](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Epoch_(astronomy)) [J2000](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/J2000)      [Equinox](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Equinox_(celestial_coordinates)) [ICRS](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/International_Celestial_Reference_System)

Details

[Astrometry](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Astrometry)

(8277±9[[2]](#cite_note-GRAVITY-2) [pc](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Parsec))

Database references
[SIMBAD](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/SIMBAD)[data](https://simbad.cds.unistra.fr/simbad/sim-id?Ident=NAME+Sgr+A*)

Sagittarius A*, [abbreviated](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Abbreviation) as Sgr A* ( [SADGE-AY-star](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Help:Pronunciation_respelling_key)[[4]](#cite_note-4)), is the [supermassive black hole](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Supermassive_black_hole)[[5]](#cite_note-5)[[6]](#cite_note-6)[[7]](#cite_note-plait-7) at the [Galactic Center](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Galactic_Center) of the [Milky Way](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Milky_Way). Viewed from Earth, it is located near the border of the constellations [Sagittarius](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Sagittarius_(constellation)) and [Scorpius](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Scorpius), about 5.6° south of the [ecliptic](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ecliptic),[[8]](#cite_note-8) visually close to the [Butterfly Cluster](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Butterfly_Cluster) (M6) and [Lambda Scorpii](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Lambda_Scorpii). Sagittarius A* is a bright and very compact [astronomical radio source](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Astronomical_radio_source).

In May 2022, astronomers released the first image of the [accretion disk](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Accretion_disk) around the event horizon of Sagittarius A*,[[9]](#cite_note-9) using the [Event Horizon Telescope](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Event_Horizon_Telescope), a world-wide network of radio observatories.[[10]](#cite_note-AJ-202205-10) This is the second confirmed image of a [black hole](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Black_hole), after [Messier 87's supermassive black hole](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Messier_87#Supermassive_black_hole_M87*) in 2019.[[11]](#cite_note-ESO2208-11)[[12]](#cite_note-NYT-20220512-12) The black hole itself is not seen; as light is incapable of escaping the immense gravitational force of a black hole, only nearby objects whose behavior is influenced by the black hole can be observed. The observed radio and infrared energy emanates from gas and dust heated to millions of degrees while falling into the black hole.[[13]](#cite_note-DetectionOfBlackHoleSrgA-13)

Sgr A* was discovered in 1974 by [Bruce Balick](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Bruce_Balick) and Robert L. Brown,[[14]](#cite_note-Intense_sub-arcsecond_structure_in-14)[[15]](#cite_note-Melia_2007,_p._7-15) and the [asterisk](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Asterisk) * was assigned in 1982 by Brown,[[16]](#cite_note-16) who understood that the strongest radio emission from the center of the galaxy appeared to be due to a compact non-thermal radio object embedded in a larger, and much brighter, radio source, [Sagittarius A](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Sagittarius_A) (Sgr A).

The observation of several stars orbiting Sagittarius A*, particularly [star S2](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/S2_(star)), have been used to determine the mass and upper limits on the radius of the object. Based on the mass and the precise radius limits obtained, astronomers concluded that Sagittarius A* was the central supermassive black hole of the Milky Way galaxy.[[17]](#cite_note-Henderson-17) The current best estimate of its mass is 4.297±0.012 million [solar masses](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Solar_mass).[[2]](#cite_note-GRAVITY-2)

[Reinhard Genzel](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Reinhard_Genzel) and [Andrea Ghez](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Andrea_M._Ghez) were each awarded a quarter-share in the 2020 [Nobel Prize in Physics](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Nobel_Prize_in_Physics) for their discovery that Sagittarius A* is a supermassive compact object, for which a black hole was the only explanation. Sir [Roger Penrose](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Roger_Penrose) received the other half "for the discovery that black hole formation is a robust prediction of the [general theory of relativity](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/General_theory_of_relativity)".[[18]](#cite_note-18)

## Observation and description
[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Sagittarius_A*&action=edit&section=1)]

https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/File:Sagittarius_Astar_in_the_constellation_of_Sagittarius.tifSagittarius A* in the constellation of Sagittarius. The black hole is marked with a red circle within the constellation of Sagittarius (The Archer). This map shows most of the stars visible to the unaided eye under good conditions.
https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/File:Eso2208-eht-mwe.tifSize comparison between Sagittarius A* and [M87*](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Messier_87#Supermassive_black_hole_M87*). The diameter of Sagittarius A* is smaller than the orbit of [Mercury](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Mercury_(planet)).
On May 12, 2022, the first image of Sagittarius A* was released by the [Event Horizon Telescope Collaboration](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Event_Horizon_Telescope_Collaboration). The image, which is based on radio interferometer data taken in 2017, confirms that the object contains a black hole. This is the second image of a black hole,[[11]](#cite_note-ESO2208-11)[[19]](#cite_note-Shadow-19) and took five years of calculations to process.[[20]](#cite_note-20) The data were collected by eight radio observatories at six geographical sites. Radio images are produced from data by [aperture synthesis](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Aperture_synthesis), usually from night-long observations of stable sources. The radio emission from Sgr A* varies on the order of minutes, complicating the analysis.[[21]](#cite_note-21)

Their result gives an overall [angular size](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Angular_diameter) for the source of 51.8±2.3 [μas](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Microarcsecond).[[19]](#cite_note-Shadow-19) At a distance of 26,000 [light-years](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Light-year) (8,000 [parsecs](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Parsec)), this yields a diameter of 51.8 million kilometres (32.2 million miles).[[a]](#cite_note-dc1-22) For comparison, Earth is 150 million [kilometres](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Kilometre) (1.0 [astronomical unit](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Astronomical_unit); 93 million [miles](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Mile)) from the [Sun](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Sun), and [Mercury](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Mercury_(planet)) is 46 million km (0.31 AU; 29 million mi) from the Sun at [perihelion](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Apsis). The [proper motion](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Proper_motion) of Sgr A* is approximately −2.70 [mas](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Minute_and_second_of_arc) per year for the [right ascension](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Right_ascension) and −5.6 mas per year for the [declination](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Declination).[[22]](#cite_note-23)[[23]](#cite_note-24)[[24]](#cite_note-25)
The telescope's measurement of these black holes tested Einstein's [theory of relativity](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Theory_of_relativity) more rigorously than has previously been done, and the results match perfectly.[[12]](#cite_note-NYT-20220512-12)

In 2019, measurements made with the High-resolution Airborne Wideband Camera-Plus (HAWC+) mounted in the [SOFIA](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Stratospheric_Observatory_for_Infrared_Astronomy) aircraft[[25]](#cite_note-26) revealed that magnetic fields cause the surrounding ring of gas and dust, temperatures of which range from −280 to 17,500 °F (99.8 to 9,977.6 K; −173.3 to 9,704.4 °C),[[26]](#cite_note-27) to flow into an orbit around Sagittarius A*, keeping black hole emissions low.[[27]](#cite_note-28)

Astronomers have been unable to observe Sgr A* in the [visible spectrum](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Visible_spectrum) because of the effect of 25 [magnitudes](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Magnitude_(astronomy)) of [extinction](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Extinction_(astronomy)) (absorption and scattering) by dust and gas between the source and Earth.[[28]](#cite_note-Osterbrock-29)

In April 1933, [Karl Jansky](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Karl_Guthe_Jansky), considered one of the fathers of radio astronomy, discovered that a radio signal was coming from a location in the direction of the constellation of Sagittarius, towards the center of the Milky Way.[[29]](#cite_note-30) The radio source later became known as [Sagittarius A](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Sagittarius_A). His observations did not extend quite as far south as the point we now know to be the Galactic Center.[[30]](#cite_note-goss_history-31) Observations by [Jack Piddington](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Jack_Piddington) and [Harry Minnett](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Harry_Clive_Minnett), using the [CSIRO](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/CSIRO) radio telescope at [Potts Hill Reservoir](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Potts_Hill_Reservoirs_1_and_2) in [Sydney](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Sydney), discovered a discrete and bright "Sagittarius-Scorpius" radio source,[[31]](#cite_note-piddington-32) which after further observation with the 80-foot (24-metre) CSIRO radio telescope at [Dover Heights](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dover_Heights) was identified in a letter to [Nature](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Nature_(journal)) as the probable Galactic Center.[[32]](#cite_note-galactic_centre_mcgee-33)

Later observations showed that Sagittarius A actually consists of several overlapping sub-components; a bright and very compact component, Sgr A*, was discovered on February 13 and 15, 1974, by Balick and Robert L. Brown using the baseline interferometer of the [National Radio Astronomy Observatory](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/National_Radio_Astronomy_Observatory).[[14]](#cite_note-Intense_sub-arcsecond_structure_in-14)[[15]](#cite_note-Melia_2007,_p._7-15) The name Sgr A* was coined by Brown in a 1982 paper because the radio source was "exciting", and [excited states](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Excited_state) of atoms are denoted with asterisks.[[33]](#cite_note-34)[[34]](#cite_note-35)

Since the 1980s, it has been evident that the central component of Sgr A* is likely a black hole. In 1994, infrared and sub-millimetre spectroscopy studies by a [Berkeley](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/University_of_California,_Berkeley) team involving [Nobel Laureate](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Nobel_Prize_in_Physics) [Charles H. Townes](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Charles_H._Townes) and future Nobel Prize Winner [Reinhard Genzel](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Reinhard_Genzel) showed that the mass of Sgr A* was tightly concentrated and on the order of 3 million Suns.[[35]](#cite_note-36)

On October 16, 2002, an international team led by [Reinhard Genzel](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Reinhard_Genzel) at the [Max Planck Institute for Extraterrestrial Physics](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Max_Planck_Institute_for_Extraterrestrial_Physics) reported the observation of the motion of the star [S2](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/S2_(star)) near Sagittarius A* throughout a period of ten years. According to the team's analysis, the data ruled out the possibility that Sgr A* contains a cluster of dark stellar objects or a mass of [degenerate fermions](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Degenerate_matter), strengthening the evidence for a massive black hole. The observations of S2 used [near-infrared](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Near-infrared) (NIR) [interferometry](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Astronomical_interferometer) (in the Ks-band, i.e. 2.1 [μm](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Μm)) because of reduced [interstellar extinction](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Extinction_(astronomy)) in this band. SiO [masers](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Astrophysical_maser) were used to align NIR images with radio observations, as they can be observed in both NIR and radio bands. The rapid motion of S2 (and other nearby stars) easily stood out against slower-moving stars along the line-of-sight so these could be subtracted from the images.[[36]](#cite_note-37)[[37]](#cite_note-38)

https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/File:Dusty_cloud_G2_passes_the_supermassive_black_hole_at_the_centre_of_the_Milky_Way.jpgDusty cloud G2 passes the supermassive black hole at the center of the Milky Way.[[38]](#cite_note-39) Composition of images taken at different times to show motion; colored blue when approaching the viewer, red when receding; time is left to right. Red cross marks the black hole.
The [VLBI](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/VLBI) radio observations of Sagittarius A* could also be aligned centrally with the NIR images, so the focus of S2's elliptical orbit was found to coincide with the position of Sagittarius A*. From examining the [Keplerian orbit](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Kepler_orbit) of S2, they determined the mass of Sagittarius A* to be 4.1±0.6 million [solar masses](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Solar_mass), confined in a volume with a radius no more than 17 light-hours (120 [AU](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Astronomical_unit) [18 [billion](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/1000000000_(number)) [km](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Kilometre); 11 billion [mi](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Mile)]).[[39]](#cite_note-40) Later observations of the star S14 showed the mass of the object to be about 4.1 million solar masses within a volume with radius no larger than 6.25 light-hours (45 AU [6.7 billion km; 4.2 billion mi]).[[40]](#cite_note-Ghez08-41) S175 passed within a similar distance.[[41]](#cite_note-42) For comparison, the [Schwarzschild radius](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Schwarzschild_radius) is 0.08 AU (12 million km; 7.4 million mi). They also determined the distance from Earth to the [Galactic Center](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Galactic_Center) (the rotational center of the Milky Way), which is important in calibrating astronomical distance scales, as 8,000 ± 600 [parsecs](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Parsec) (30,000 ± 2,000 [light-years](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Light-year)). In November 2004, a team of astronomers reported the discovery of a potential [intermediate-mass black hole](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Intermediate-mass_black_hole), referred to as [GCIRS 13E](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/GCIRS_13E), orbiting 3 light-years from Sagittarius A*. This black hole of 1,300 solar masses is within a cluster of seven stars. This observation may add support to the idea that supermassive black holes grow by absorbing nearby smaller black holes and stars.[[citation needed](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed)]

After monitoring stellar orbits around Sagittarius A* for 16 years, Gillessen et al. estimated the object's mass at 4.31±0.38 million solar masses. The result was announced in 2008 and published in [The Astrophysical Journal](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/The_Astrophysical_Journal) in 2009.[[42]](#cite_note-Gillessen-43) [Reinhard Genzel](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Reinhard_Genzel), team leader of the research, said the study has delivered "what is now considered to be the best empirical evidence that supermassive black holes do really exist. The stellar orbits in the Galactic Center show that the central mass concentration of four million solar masses must be a black hole, beyond any reasonable doubt."[[43]](#cite_note-44)

On January 5, 2015, NASA reported observing an [X-ray](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/X-ray) flare 400 times brighter than usual, a record-breaker, from Sgr A*. The unusual event may have been caused by the breaking apart of an [asteroid](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Asteroid) falling into the black hole or by the entanglement of [magnetic field](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Magnetic_field) lines within gas flowing into Sgr A*, according to astronomers.[[44]](#cite_note-NASA-20150105-45)

On May 13, 2019, astronomers using the [Keck Observatory](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Keck_Observatory) witnessed a sudden brightening of Sgr A*, which became 75 times brighter than usual, suggesting that the supermassive black hole may have encountered another object.[[45]](#cite_note-46)

In June 2023, unexplained filaments of [radio](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Radio_wave) energy were found associated with Sagittarius A*.[[46]](#cite_note-NYT-20230608-47)

- https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/File:Cloudlets_swarm_around_our_local_supermassive_black_hole.tif
[ALMA](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Atacama_Large_Millimeter_Array) observations of molecular-hydrogen-rich gas clouds, with the area around Sagittarius A* circled[[47]](#cite_note-48)

- https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/File:X-RayFlare-BlackHole-MilkyWay-20140105.jpg
An unusually bright [X-ray](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/X-ray) flare from Sgr A* was detected in 2013.[[44]](#cite_note-NASA-20150105-45)

- https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/File:15-044a-SuperNovaRemnant-PlanetFormation-SOFIA-20150319.jpg
[Supernova remnant](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Supernova_remnant) ejecta producing planet-forming material seen in radio and X-ray

- https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/File:15-044b-SuperNovaRemnant-PlanetFormation-SOFIA-20150319.jpg
Supernova remnant ejecta producing planet-forming material seen in infrared and X-ray

https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/File:Pointing_X-ray_Eyes_at_our_Resident_Supermassive_Black_Hole.jpg[NuSTAR](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/NuSTAR) has captured these first, focused views of the supermassive black hole at the heart of the Milky Way in high-energy X-rays.In a paper published on October 31, 2018, the discovery of conclusive evidence that Sagittarius A* is a black hole was announced. Using the [GRAVITY](https://en.wikipedia.or

Based on the Wikipedia article, the current best estimate for the mass of Sagittarius A* is:

**4.297 ± 0.012 million solar masses** (approximately 4.3 million times the mass of our Sun)

This measurement comes from the GRAVITY collaboration's observations of stellar orbits, particularly the star S2. In more familiar units, that's about **8.54 × 10³⁶ kg** — a truly incomprehensible amount of mass packed into a region smaller than Mercury's orbit around the Sun.

The 2020 Nobel Prize in Physics was awarded to Reinhard Genzel and Andrea Ghez for their work establishing that Sagittarius A* is indeed a supermassive black hole. In 2022, the Event Horizon Telescope captured the first direct image of its accretion disk, confirming the black hole's existence and providing additional data to constrain its properties.
